cards.definition: the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms
a cell with a true nucleus enclosed by a membrane — includes plant and animal cells
a cell with no membrane-bound nucleus — bacteria are prokaryotes
a phospholipid bilayer controlling what enters and leaves the cell — semi-permeable
contains DNA as chromosomes — controls cell activities — only in eukaryotes
site of aerobic respiration — produce ATP — found in eukaryotes
site of protein synthesis — found in all cells including prokaryotes
contains chlorophyll — site of photosynthesis — in plant cells only
rigid cellulose layer outside cell membrane — provides support and shape
made of peptidoglycan — different from plant cell walls — prevents bursting

questions.question: What is the key difference between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells?
options: A) Size only
B) Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus; prokaryotes do not
C) Prokaryotes have more organelles
D) Only prokaryotes have DNA
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells have a true nucleus enclosed by a nuclear envelope. Prokaryotic cells have no membrane-bound nucleus — DNA floats in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: A) Provides rigid support
B) Controls what enters and leaves — semi-permeable phospholipid bilayer
C) Contains DNA
D) Carries out photosynthesis
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins — it controls movement of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: Which organelle is found in plant cells but not animal cells?
options: A) Mitochondria
B) Ribosomes
C) Nucleus
D) Chloroplast
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis — found only in plant cells.

questions.question: What is the function of ribosomes?
options: A) Aerobic respiration
B) Photosynthesis
C) Protein synthesis
D) Storing genetic information
questions.explanation: Ribosomes synthesise proteins by translating mRNA — they are found in ALL cells, both eukaryotic and prokaryotic.

questions.question: What is the plant cell wall made from?
options: A) Peptidoglycan
B) Chitin
C) Cellulose
D) Lignin
questions.explanation: Plant cell walls are made of cellulose — a structural carbohydrate providing rigidity and support.

questions.question: What is the function of mitochondria?
options: A) Protein synthesis
B) Photosynthesis
C) Aerobic respiration — producing ATP
D) Controlling cell activities
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration — they produce ATP, the energy currency of the cell.

questions.question: What is the bacterial cell wall made from?
options: A) Cellulose
B) Peptidoglycan
C) Chitin
D) No cell wall
questions.explanation: Bacterial cell walls are made of peptidoglycan — many antibiotics target its synthesis.

questions.question: What does the nucleus contain?
options: A) Ribosomes and ATP
B) Chlorophyll
C) DNA as chromosomes — controls cell activities
D) Mitochondria
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains chromosomes (DNA + protein). It controls the cell by directing protein synthesis.

questions.question: Which organelles are found in both plant and animal cells?
options: A) Chloroplasts and vacuoles
B) Nucleus, mitochondria, and ribosomes
C) Cell walls and chloroplasts
D) Vacuoles only
questions.explanation: Both plant and animal cells have nucleus (genetic control), mitochondria (respiration), and ribosomes (protein synthesis).

questions.question: What is a prokaryotic cell?
options: A) A cell with a large nucleus
B) A cell with multiple nuclei
C) A cell with no membrane-bound nucleus — e.g. bacteria
D) A cell without ribosomes
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells have no membrane-bound nucleus — DNA is a single circular chromosome in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: Eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus.
questions.explanation: Eukaryotes (plants, animals, fungi) have a true nucleus enclosed by a nuclear membrane.

questions.question: Prokaryotic cells are larger than eukaryotic cells.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells are typically SMALLER than eukaryotic cells.

questions.question: Ribosomes are found in all cells.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes synthesise proteins — they are found in both eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells.

questions.question: Chloroplasts are found in all plant and animal cells.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are found only in PLANT cells — not in animal cells.

questions.question: Bacterial cell walls are made of cellulose.
questions.explanation: Bacterial cell walls are made of PEPTIDOGLYCAN — plant cell walls are made of cellulose.

questions.question: Mitochondria produce ATP through aerobic respiration.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the site of aerobic respiration — producing the majority of a cell's ATP.

questions.question: Both plant and animal cells have a cell wall.
questions.explanation: Only plant cells (and fungi and bacteria) have cell walls. Animal cells have only a cell membrane.

questions.question: The nucleus controls cell activities.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA — it controls the cell by directing which proteins are made.

questions.question: Prokaryotic cells have no membrane-bound nucleus.
questions.explanation: In prokaryotes, DNA is a circular chromosome in the cytoplasm — not enclosed by a membrane.

questions.question: Plant cell walls are made of peptidoglycan.
questions.explanation: Plant cell walls are made of CELLULOSE. BACTERIAL cell walls are made of peptidoglycan.

questions.question: ________ cells have a membrane-bound nucleus — plants and animals.
questions.explanation: Eukaryotes also have other membrane-bound organelles.

questions.question: ________ cells have no membrane-bound nucleus — bacteria.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic DNA is a single circular chromosome in the cytoplasm.

questions.question: The cell ________ is a semi-permeable phospholipid bilayer controlling substance movement.
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is present in ALL cells.

questions.question: ________ are the site of aerobic respiration — producing ATP.
questions.explanation: More metabolically active cells have more mitochondria.

questions.question: ________ are the site of protein synthesis — found in all cells.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes translate mRNA into proteins.

questions.question: ________ contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are only found in plant cells.

questions.question: Plant cell walls are made of ________.
questions.explanation: Cellulose provides structural rigidity to plant cells.

questions.question: Bacterial cell walls are made of ________.
questions.explanation: Peptidoglycan is the target of many antibiotics.

questions.question: The ________ contains DNA as chromosomes and controls cell activities.
questions.explanation: Only eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus.

questions.question: ________ are plant organelles containing chlorophyll for photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts have a double membrane and contain thylakoids stacked in grana.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: membrane-bound nucleus — plants and animals
no membrane-bound nucleus — bacteria
site of aerobic respiration — produces ATP
site of protein synthesis — all cells
photosynthesis — plant cells only

cards.definition: net movement of particles from high to low concentration — passive, no energy needed
diffusion of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane — from dilute to concentrated solution
movement of substances against a concentration gradient — requires energy (ATP)
the difference in concentration between two areas — drives diffusion
a membrane that allows some molecules to pass but not others — e.g. cell membrane
pressure exerted by water inside a plant cell against the cell wall — keeps plant firm
when a plant cell loses water by osmosis and the cell membrane pulls away from the cell wall
as cells increase in size, SA:V ratio decreases — affects rate of diffusion into/out of cell
diffusion through protein channels in the membrane — passive, no energy needed
membrane proteins that transport specific molecules by active transport or facilitated diffusion

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: A) Movement against a concentration gradient requiring energy
B) Net movement of particles from high to low concentration — passive
C) Movement of water through a membrane
D) Transport requiring ATP
questions.explanation: Diffusion is passive — particles move down their concentration gradient until evenly distributed.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: A) Active transport of ions
B) Movement of any molecules through a membrane
C) Diffusion of water through a partially permeable membrane — dilute to concentrated
D) Movement of glucose into cells
questions.explanation: Osmosis is specifically water movement — from a dilute (high water potential) to concentrated (low water potential) solution through a partially permeable membrane.

questions.question: What is active transport?
options: A) Fast diffusion
B) Movement of substances against a concentration gradient — requires ATP
C) Osmosis of water molecules
D) Diffusion through protein channels
questions.explanation: Active transport moves substances from low to high concentration — against the gradient. It requires ATP and carrier proteins.

questions.question: What is turgor pressure?
options: A) Pressure from osmosis in animal cells
B) Pressure from water inside a plant cell pushing against the cell wall
C) The force needed for active transport
D) Pressure from plasmolysis
questions.explanation: Turgor pressure keeps plants firm and upright — when plant cells are full of water they are turgid. Water loss causes wilting.

questions.question: What is plasmolysis?
options: A) Cell bursting due to excess water
B) Plant cell losing water — cell membrane pulls away from cell wall
C) Active transport in plant cells
D) Water entering plant cells
questions.explanation: Plasmolysis occurs when a plant cell is placed in a concentrated solution — water leaves by osmosis and the membrane shrinks away from the wall.

questions.question: Why does surface area to volume ratio matter for cells?
options: A) Larger cells have higher SA:V — better diffusion
B) SA:V ratio is irrelevant to cell function
C) As cells grow, SA:V decreases — diffusion becomes less efficient
D) SA:V only matters for plant cells
questions.explanation: As cells get larger, the volume increases faster than surface area — diffusion distances increase and exchange becomes less efficient. This limits cell size.

questions.question: What is facilitated diffusion?
options: A) Diffusion requiring ATP
B) Diffusion through protein channels — passive, no energy needed
C) Active transport through carrier proteins
D) Osmosis through aquaporin channels
questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion uses protein channels — it is passive (no ATP needed) but is specific to certain molecules that cannot cross the lipid bilayer directly.

questions.question: What is a concentration gradient?
options: A) The speed at which particles move
B) The difference in concentration between two areas — drives diffusion
C) The concentration of a solution
D) A gradient caused by active transport
questions.explanation: The steeper the concentration gradient, the faster the rate of diffusion — more particles move from high to low concentration.

questions.question: What happens to a plant cell placed in a dilute solution?
options: A) It plasmolysis
B) It shrinks
C) It becomes turgid — water enters by osmosis
D) Nothing changes
questions.explanation: In a dilute solution (higher water potential than the cell), water enters by osmosis — the cell becomes turgid and the cell wall prevents it from bursting.

questions.question: How does active transport differ from diffusion?
options: A) Active transport moves with the gradient
B) Diffusion requires ATP
C) Active transport moves against the gradient and requires ATP; diffusion is passive
D) They are the same process
questions.explanation: Diffusion: passive, with the gradient. Active transport: requires ATP, against the gradient. Both can use membrane proteins.

questions.question: Diffusion requires energy (ATP).
questions.explanation: Diffusion is PASSIVE — particles move down their concentration gradient without energy input.

questions.question: Osmosis is the diffusion of water through a partially permeable membrane.
questions.explanation: Water moves from dilute (high water potential) to concentrated (low water potential) solution.

questions.question: Active transport moves substances against their concentration gradient.
questions.explanation: Active transport requires ATP and carrier proteins to move substances from low to high concentration.

questions.question: Turgor pressure keeps plant cells firm.
questions.explanation: Water entering by osmosis creates turgor pressure — the cell wall resists the expansion, keeping the plant rigid.

questions.question: Plasmolysis occurs when a plant cell gains water.
questions.explanation: Plasmolysis occurs when a plant cell LOSES water — the membrane pulls away from the cell wall.

questions.question: As a cell gets larger, its SA:V ratio increases.
questions.explanation: As cells grow, the SA:V ratio DECREASES — volume grows faster than surface area.

questions.question: Facilitated diffusion requires ATP.
questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion is PASSIVE — it uses protein channels but requires no energy.

questions.question: Diffusion moves particles from low to high concentration.
questions.explanation: Diffusion moves particles from HIGH to LOW concentration — down the gradient.

questions.question: A plant cell in a concentrated solution will become turgid.
questions.explanation: A plant cell in a concentrated solution will LOSE water by osmosis — it will plasmolyse, not become turgid.

questions.question: Active transport and diffusion both use membrane proteins.
questions.explanation: Diffusion can use protein channels (facilitated diffusion); active transport always uses carrier proteins.

questions.question: ________ is the net movement of particles from high to low concentration — no energy needed.
questions.explanation: Diffusion is a passive process driven by the concentration gradient.

questions.question: ________ is the diffusion of water through a partially permeable membrane.
questions.explanation: Water moves from dilute to concentrated solution.

questions.question: ________ moves substances against a concentration gradient — requires ATP.
questions.explanation: Active transport uses carrier proteins and ATP.

questions.question: ________ pressure is water pressing against the plant cell wall — keeps plants firm.
questions.explanation: Loss of turgor pressure causes wilting.

questions.question: ________ occurs when a plant cell loses water and the membrane pulls away from the wall.
questions.explanation: Caused by placing a plant cell in a concentrated solution.

questions.question: As cells increase in size, surface area to ________ ratio decreases.
questions.explanation: Decreasing SA:V ratio reduces efficiency of diffusion.

questions.question: ________ diffusion uses protein channels — passive, no ATP needed.
questions.explanation: Molecules too large or charged to cross the lipid bilayer use protein channels.

questions.question: The ________ gradient drives diffusion — steeper gradient = faster diffusion.
questions.explanation: Particles move from areas of high to low concentration.

questions.question: A plant cell in a dilute solution absorbs water by osmosis and becomes ________.
questions.explanation: The cell wall prevents bursting — turgor pressure builds up.

questions.question: ________ proteins transport specific molecules across the membrane in active transport.
questions.explanation: Carrier proteins change shape to move molecules against the gradient.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: high to low concentration — passive
water through partially permeable membrane — dilute to concentrated
against gradient — requires ATP
water pressure in plant cell — keeps plant firm
plant cell loses water — membrane pulls from wall

cards.definition: cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells — for growth and repair
cell division producing four genetically different gametes — halves chromosome number
a long strand of DNA carrying genes — humans have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs
a section of DNA that codes for a protein — determines an inherited characteristic
a version of a gene — e.g. the gene for eye colour has alleles for blue and brown
an allele whose characteristic is always expressed — even if only one copy is present
an allele only expressed if two copies are present (homozygous recessive)
the genetic make-up of an organism — the alleles it carries
the observable characteristics of an organism — result of genotype and environment
an undifferentiated cell that can divide and differentiate into specialised cell types

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: A) Cell division producing gametes
B) Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
C) Cell division halving chromosome number
D) Cell division for sexual reproduction
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two identical cells — used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction. The daughter cells are genetically identical to the parent.

questions.question: What is meiosis?
options: A) Cell division for growth
B) Cell division producing identical cells
C) Cell division producing four genetically different gametes — halves chromosome number
D) Cell division in prokaryotes
questions.explanation: Meiosis occurs in the reproductive organs — producing gametes (sperm/eggs) with half the chromosome number (haploid).

questions.question: What is a dominant allele?
options: A) An allele only expressed when two copies are present
B) An allele always expressed — even with just one copy
C) An allele that causes disease
D) The most common allele in a population
questions.explanation: Dominant alleles are expressed whenever they are present — one copy is enough to show the characteristic.

questions.question: What is the difference between genotype and phenotype?
options: A) They are the same thing
B) Genotype is observable; phenotype is genetic
C) Genotype is the alleles carried; phenotype is the observable characteristic
D) Genotype only refers to dominant alleles
questions.explanation: Genotype = genetic makeup (alleles). Phenotype = observable result — influenced by both genotype and environment.

questions.question: What is a stem cell?
options: A) A cell that cannot divide
B) A specialised muscle cell
C) An undifferentiated cell that can divide and become specialised
D) A cell found only in plants
questions.explanation: Stem cells are undifferentiated — they can self-renew and differentiate into many cell types. Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent.

questions.question: How many chromosomes do human body cells contain?
questions.explanation: Human body cells have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs — one from each parent. Gametes have 23 (haploid).

questions.question: What is a recessive allele?
options: A) Always expressed
B) Only expressed when two copies are present
C) The dominant form of a gene
D) An allele that causes cancer
questions.explanation: Recessive alleles are only expressed in the homozygous recessive genotype (aa) — they are masked by dominant alleles.

questions.question: What is a gene?
options: A) A complete chromosome
B) All of an organism's DNA
C) A section of DNA coding for a specific protein
D) A type of allele
questions.explanation: A gene is a specific sequence of DNA bases that codes for the production of a particular protein.

questions.question: What is the purpose of meiosis?
options: A) Growth and repair
B) Asexual reproduction
C) Producing gametes with half the chromosome number for sexual reproduction
D) Replacing damaged cells
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces haploid gametes (sperm and eggs) — when they fuse at fertilisation, the full diploid number is restored.

questions.question: What happens during differentiation?
options: A) Cells lose their DNA
B) Cells become specialised by switching on specific genes
C) Cells divide by mitosis
D) Cells die
questions.explanation: During differentiation, cells activate specific genes — producing the proteins needed for their specialised function (e.g. muscle cells produce actin/myosin).

questions.question: Mitosis produces genetically identical daughter cells.
questions.explanation: Mitosis is used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction — the daughter cells are identical to the parent.

questions.question: Meiosis produces two daughter cells.
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces FOUR genetically different daughter cells (gametes).

questions.question: A dominant allele is only expressed when two copies are present.
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed with just ONE copy — only recessive alleles require two copies.

questions.question: Phenotype is determined solely by genotype.
questions.explanation: Phenotype is influenced by BOTH genotype and environmental factors.

questions.question: Stem cells are undifferentiated and can become specialised.
questions.explanation: Stem cells can self-renew and differentiate into specialised cell types.

questions.question: Human gametes contain 46 chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Human GAMETES contain 23 chromosomes (haploid). Body cells contain 46.

questions.question: A recessive allele is expressed in a heterozygous genotype.
questions.explanation: Recessive alleles are only expressed when HOMOZYGOUS (aa) — they are masked in heterozygotes.

questions.question: Genes are sections of DNA coding for proteins.
questions.explanation: Each gene contains the code for the sequence of amino acids in a specific protein.

questions.question: Meiosis occurs in body cells for growth.
questions.explanation: MITOSIS occurs for growth. Meiosis occurs in reproductive organs to produce gametes.

questions.question: Differentiation involves cells switching on specific genes.
questions.explanation: Differentiation results from specific genes being expressed — producing the proteins needed for the cell's specialised role.

questions.question: ________ produces two genetically identical daughter cells — for growth and repair.
questions.explanation: Mitosis is also used for asexual reproduction.

questions.question: ________ produces four genetically different gametes — halves chromosome number.
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces haploid cells (sperm and eggs).

questions.question: A ________ allele is always expressed — even with just one copy.
questions.explanation: Dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygotes.

questions.question: A ________ allele is only expressed when two copies are present.
questions.explanation: Recessive alleles are masked in heterozygous individuals.

questions.question: ________ is the genetic make-up of an organism — the alleles it carries.
questions.explanation: Genotype is written as pairs of letters — e.g. Bb.

questions.question: ________ is the observable characteristic — result of genotype and environment.
questions.explanation: E.g. tall or short, brown or blue eyes.

questions.question: ________ cells are undifferentiated and can become specialised cell types.
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent — can become any cell type.

questions.question: Humans have ________ chromosomes in body cells — 23 pairs.
questions.explanation: Gametes have 23 chromosomes (haploid).

questions.question: A ________ is a section of DNA coding for a specific protein.
questions.explanation: Genes are found at specific locations (loci) on chromosomes.

questions.question: ________ is a version of a gene — e.g. blue or brown eye colour.
questions.explanation: Alleles for the same gene are found at the same locus on homologous chromosomes.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: two identical daughter cells — growth and repair
four different gametes — halves chromosome number
expressed with one copy — masks recessive
only expressed with two copies
undifferentiated — can become specialised

cards.definition: maintaining a constant internal environment — e.g. temperature, blood glucose, water levels
a mechanism that reverses a change to restore normal levels — the basis of homeostasis
control of body temperature — the hypothalamus detects and responds to temperature changes
insulin lowers blood glucose; glucagon raises it — both secreted by the pancreas
hormone secreted by the pancreas — causes cells to absorb glucose, lowering blood glucose
hormone secreted by the pancreas — stimulates glycogen breakdown, raising blood glucose
hormone controlling water reabsorption in the kidney — released when blood is too concentrated
the brain, spinal cord, and nerves — rapid electrical communication using neurones
a rapid, automatic response — receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
the gap between two neurones — neurotransmitters diffuse across to pass the signal

questions.question: What is homeostasis?
options: A) Growth of an organism
B) Maintaining a constant internal environment
C) Responding to external stimuli
D) Regulation of reproduction
questions.explanation: Homeostasis maintains stable conditions — temperature, blood glucose, water content — essential for enzyme function and cell processes.

questions.question: What is negative feedback?
options: A) A mechanism that amplifies a change
B) A mechanism that reverses a change to restore normal levels
C) A type of hormonal response
D) The immune response to pathogens
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: if a variable rises above the set point, a response brings it back down. If it falls below, a response raises it. This maintains stability.

questions.question: What is the role of insulin?
options: A) Raises blood glucose
B) Stimulates glycogen breakdown
C) Lowers blood glucose — causes cells to absorb glucose
D) Controls water reabsorption
questions.explanation: Insulin is released when blood glucose is too high — it causes cells to absorb glucose and stimulates the liver to convert glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis).

questions.question: What is the role of glucagon?
options: A) Lowers blood glucose
B) Stimulates glycogen storage
C) Raises blood glucose — stimulates glycogen breakdown in the liver
D) Controls temperature
questions.explanation: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is too low — it stimulates the liver to break down glycogen to glucose (glycogenolysis).

questions.question: What is ADH and what does it do?
options: A) A hormone that raises blood glucose
B) A hormone that controls heart rate
C) A hormone that increases water reabsorption in the kidney — released when blood is concentrated
D) A neurotransmitter
questions.explanation: ADH (antidiuretic hormone) is released by the pituitary gland when blood water content is low — it causes more water to be reabsorbed in the kidney tubules.

questions.question: What is a reflex arc?
options: A) A voluntary response to a stimulus
B) A rapid, automatic response — receptor → sensory → relay → motor neurone → effector
C) A slow hormonal response
D) A learned behaviour
questions.explanation: Reflex arcs bypass the brain — the signal passes through the spinal cord for a faster response, protecting the body from harm.

questions.question: What is a synapse?
options: A) A type of neurone
B) The gap between two neurones — neurotransmitters carry the signal across
C) A receptor detecting stimuli
D) The part of the brain controlling reflexes
questions.explanation: At a synapse, neurotransmitters (e.g. acetylcholine) diffuse across the gap and bind to receptors on the next neurone, restarting the electrical signal.

questions.question: What does the hypothalamus do?
options: A) Produces hormones that enter the blood
B) Detects changes in body temperature and blood composition — coordinates responses
C) Controls voluntary movement
D) Produces insulin
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus is the body's control centre for homeostasis — it detects changes and coordinates responses via the nervous and endocrine systems.

questions.question: What is thermoregulation?
options: A) Control of blood glucose
B) Control of body temperature — the hypothalamus detects temperature changes
C) Control of water balance
D) Control of breathing rate
questions.explanation: Thermoregulation: if too hot — vasodilation, sweating; if too cold — vasoconstriction, shivering. The hypothalamus coordinates all these responses.

questions.question: How does the nervous system communicate?
options: A) Via hormones in the blood
B) Via rapid electrical impulses along neurones
C) Via diffusion of neurotransmitters throughout the body
D) Via changes in gene expression
questions.explanation: The nervous system uses electrical impulses — fast and short-lived. The endocrine system uses hormones — slower but longer-lasting.

questions.question: Homeostasis maintains a constant internal environment.
questions.explanation: Homeostasis uses negative feedback to keep temperature, blood glucose, and water content stable.

questions.question: Negative feedback amplifies changes away from the set point.
questions.explanation: Negative feedback REVERSES changes — bringing variables back to the set point.

questions.question: Insulin raises blood glucose.
questions.explanation: Insulin LOWERS blood glucose — it causes cells to absorb glucose and stimulates glycogen storage.

questions.question: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is too high.
questions.explanation: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is TOO LOW — it raises glucose by breaking down glycogen.

questions.question: ADH increases water reabsorption in the kidney.
questions.explanation: ADH is released when blood is too concentrated — it causes more water to be reabsorbed.

questions.question: Reflex arcs involve conscious decision-making in the brain.
questions.explanation: Reflex arcs BYPASS the brain — the signal travels through the spinal cord for a faster response.

questions.question: Neurotransmitters carry signals across synapses.
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse across the synaptic cleft and bind to receptors on the next neurone.

questions.question: The hypothalamus controls homeostasis.
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus detects changes in body conditions and coordinates corrective responses.

questions.question: Insulin and glucagon are both secreted by the pancreas.
questions.explanation: The islets of Langerhans in the pancreas contain beta cells (insulin) and alpha cells (glucagon).

questions.question: The nervous system communicates using hormones in the blood.
questions.explanation: The nervous system uses ELECTRICAL IMPULSES along neurones — hormones are used by the ENDOCRINE system.

questions.question: ________ maintains a constant internal environment — temperature, glucose, water.
questions.explanation: Homeostasis uses negative feedback loops.

questions.question: ________ feedback reverses changes — restoring normal levels.
questions.explanation: The basis of all homeostatic control.

questions.question: ________ lowers blood glucose — secreted by the pancreas.
questions.explanation: Insulin causes cells to absorb glucose and stimulates glycogen storage.

questions.question: ________ raises blood glucose by stimulating glycogen breakdown.
questions.explanation: Glucagon is released when blood glucose falls too low.

questions.question: ________ increases water reabsorption in the kidney when blood is concentrated.
questions.explanation: ADH stands for antidiuretic hormone.

questions.question: A ________ arc is a rapid, automatic response bypassing the brain.
questions.explanation: Receptor → sensory → relay → motor neurone → effector.

questions.question: A ________ is the gap between neurones — neurotransmitters carry the signal across.
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse across and bind to receptors on the next neurone.

questions.question: The ________ detects temperature changes and coordinates homeostatic responses.
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus is the body's main homeostasis control centre.

questions.question: The nervous system uses ________ impulses along neurones.
questions.explanation: Nerve impulses are fast and short-lived compared to hormonal responses.

questions.question: ________ is the control of body temperature — responses include sweating and shivering.
questions.explanation: Too hot: vasodilation and sweating. Too cold: vasoconstriction and shivering.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: constant internal environment via negative feedback
lowers blood glucose — secreted by pancreas
raises blood glucose — stimulates glycogen breakdown
increases water reabsorption in kidney
receptor → sensory → relay → motor → effector

cards.definition: a double helix polymer of nucleotides — carries the genetic code for all proteins
A pairs with T; C pairs with G — complementary base pairs hold the DNA strands together
a change in the sequence of DNA bases — can alter the protein produced
organisms with advantageous adaptations survive and reproduce — passing on their genes
a feature that improves an organism's chance of survival in its environment
the formation of a new species — occurs when populations become reproductively isolated
a species no longer exists — can be caused by habitat loss, disease, competition
differences between individuals — caused by genetic differences, environmental factors, or both
a grid used to predict the probability of offspring genotypes from a genetic cross
sex is determined by sex chromosomes — XX = female, XY = male

questions.question: What is the base pairing rule in DNA?
options: A) A pairs with G; T pairs with C
B) A pairs with T; C pairs with G
C) A pairs with C; T pairs with G
D) All bases pair with each other
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing: Adenine-Thymine and Cytosine-Guanine. This holds the double helix together.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: A) Humans selecting the best organisms to breed
B) Random changes in DNA
C) Organisms with advantageous adaptations surviving and reproducing — passing on genes
D) The process of speciation only
questions.explanation: Natural selection: variation exists → some variants survive better → they reproduce more → advantageous alleles become more common over generations.

questions.question: What causes speciation?
options: A) Mutation alone
B) Natural selection alone
C) Reproductive isolation — separated populations evolve differently until they can no longer interbreed
D) Adaptation to different diets
questions.explanation: Speciation requires reproductive isolation — separated populations accumulate different mutations and adaptations until they become distinct species.

questions.question: What is a mutation?
options: A) A change in the organism's environment
B) A change in the organism's behaviour
C) A change in the DNA base sequence
D) A change caused by natural selection
questions.explanation: Mutations are changes in DNA sequence — most are neutral, some are harmful, occasionally they are beneficial. They are the source of new alleles.

questions.question: What determines biological sex in humans?
options: A) Hormones only
B) X and Y chromosomes — XX = female, XY = male
C) The number of autosomes
D) Environmental factors
questions.explanation: The sex chromosomes determine biological sex: females have two X chromosomes; males have one X and one Y chromosome.

questions.question: What is an adaptation?
options: A) A change in DNA
B) Learning a new behaviour
C) A feature improving an organism's chance of survival in its environment
D) A type of mutation
questions.explanation: Adaptations can be structural (e.g. thick fur), behavioural (e.g. migration), or physiological (e.g. producing antifreeze proteins).

questions.question: What is a Punnett square used for?
options: A) Drawing DNA structures
B) Predicting offspring genotype probabilities from a genetic cross
C) Mapping chromosomes
D) Calculating mutation rates
questions.explanation: Punnett squares show all possible combinations of alleles from two parents — each box represents a 25% probability.

questions.question: What causes variation within a species?
options: A) Only genetic differences
B) Only environmental factors
C) Genetic differences, environmental factors, or both
D) Only mutations
questions.explanation: Continuous variation (e.g. height) is influenced by multiple genes and the environment. Discontinuous variation (e.g. blood group) is determined by one gene.

questions.question: What can cause extinction?
options: A) Successful adaptation only
B) Habitat loss, disease, competition, climate change — a species is lost forever
C) Natural selection alone
D) Speciation only
questions.explanation: Extinction is permanent — causes include habitat destruction, introduced predators, disease, climate change, and human overexploitation.

questions.question: What is DNA?
options: A) A single-stranded molecule of amino acids
B) A double helix polymer of nucleotides — carries the genetic code
C) A type of RNA
D) A protein molecule
questions.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a double helix — the sequence of bases codes for the order of amino acids in proteins.

questions.question: In DNA, A pairs with T and C pairs with G.
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing holds the two DNA strands together.

questions.question: Natural selection requires organisms to deliberately adapt.
questions.explanation: Natural selection is not deliberate — organisms with existing advantageous variations survive and reproduce more.

questions.question: Speciation requires reproductive isolation.
questions.explanation: Populations must be isolated so they cannot interbreed — over time they accumulate different adaptations and become separate species.

questions.question: Mutations always produce harmful changes.
questions.explanation: Most mutations are neutral. Some are harmful. Occasionally mutations are beneficial — providing new variation for natural selection.

questions.question: Human females have XY sex chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Human females have XX chromosomes. Males have XY.

questions.question: Adaptations improve an organism's chance of survival.
questions.explanation: Adaptations can be structural, behavioural, or physiological — all increase survival and reproduction in a particular environment.

questions.question: Punnett squares can predict exact offspring genotypes.
questions.explanation: Punnett squares predict PROBABILITIES — not certainties. Each offspring is an independent event.

questions.question: Variation within a species can be caused by environmental factors.
questions.explanation: Environment affects phenotype — e.g. a genetically tall person may be shorter if malnourished.

questions.question: Extinction means all members of a species have died.
questions.explanation: Extinction is the permanent loss of a species — it cannot be reversed.

questions.question: DNA base sequence codes for amino acid sequence in proteins.
questions.explanation: The order of DNA bases determines the order of amino acids in the protein produced.

questions.question: ________ is a double helix of nucleotides — carries the genetic code.
questions.explanation: The sequence of DNA bases codes for the sequence of amino acids in proteins.

questions.question: Base pairing: A pairs with ________ ; C pairs with G.
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing holds the double helix together.

questions.question: A ________ is a change in DNA base sequence — can alter the protein produced.
questions.explanation: Mutations are the source of new alleles and genetic variation.

questions.question: ________ selection: organisms with advantageous adaptations survive and reproduce.
questions.explanation: Over generations, advantageous alleles become more common in the population.

questions.question: ________ occurs when populations become reproductively isolated and diverge.
questions.explanation: New species form when isolated populations can no longer interbreed.

questions.question: An ________ is a feature improving survival in a particular environment.
questions.explanation: Adaptations can be structural, behavioural, or physiological.

questions.question: ________ is the permanent loss of a species — cannot be reversed.
questions.explanation: Caused by habitat loss, disease, competition, or climate change.

questions.question: A ________ square predicts probability of offspring genotypes from a genetic cross.
questions.explanation: Each box in a Punnett square represents a 25% probability.

questions.question: Human sex is determined by sex chromosomes — ________ = female, XY = male.
questions.explanation: The Y chromosome carries the SRY gene that triggers male development.

questions.question: ________ is differences between individuals — genetic, environmental, or both.
questions.explanation: Variation provides the raw material for natural selection.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: advantageous adaptations → survive → reproduce → genes passed on
change in DNA sequence — source of new alleles
reproductive isolation → populations diverge → new species
feature improving survival in environment
A-T, C-G — holds double helix together

cards.definition: all the living organisms in an area and the non-living environment they interact with
shows feeding relationships — producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → tertiary consumer
an organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis — the first trophic level
an organism that eats other organisms — herbivores eat plants, carnivores eat animals
organisms that break down dead matter — fungi and bacteria — recycle nutrients
the movement of carbon through living organisms, the atmosphere, and the ground
the movement of nitrogen through living organisms and soil — nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification
the variety of different species in an ecosystem — high biodiversity increases stability
contamination of the environment by human activities — e.g. acid rain, eutrophication, plastic
excess fertiliser runs into water → algal bloom → oxygen depletion → death of aquatic organisms

questions.question: What is an ecosystem?
options: A) All the plants in an area
B) The non-living environment only
C) All living organisms and their non-living environment in an area
D) A food web
questions.explanation: An ecosystem includes biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components that interact — e.g. a pond, a woodland, a coral reef.

questions.question: What is the role of decomposers?
options: A) Photosynthesis
B) Predation
C) Breaking down dead matter — recycling nutrients back into the soil
D) Nitrogen fixation only
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and waste — releasing nutrients that can be reabsorbed by plants.

questions.question: What is eutrophication?
options: A) Loss of biodiversity from habitat destruction
B) Excess fertiliser → algal bloom → oxygen depletion → death of aquatic life
C) Acid rain damage to forests
D) Greenhouse effect causing warming
questions.explanation: Eutrophication: fertilisers wash into water → algae bloom → algae block sunlight → submerged plants die → bacteria decompose plants → bacteria use oxygen → aquatic organisms die.

questions.question: What is biodiversity?
options: A) The number of individuals in one species
B) The variety of different species in an ecosystem
C) The number of food chains in an ecosystem
D) The total biomass of an ecosystem
questions.explanation: High biodiversity increases ecosystem stability — more species means more feeding relationships and better resilience to change.

questions.question: What is the carbon cycle?
options: A) Carbon moving only through the atmosphere
B) Carbon moving through living organisms, atmosphere, and the ground
C) Carbon being released only by combustion
D) Carbon only in photosynthesis and respiration
questions.explanation: Carbon cycles through photosynthesis (CO₂ absorbed), respiration (CO₂ released), decomposition, and combustion.

questions.question: What is a producer?
options: A) An organism that eats plants
B) An organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis — the first trophic level
C) A decomposer
D) A tertiary consumer
questions.explanation: Producers (green plants and algae) convert light energy into chemical energy via photosynthesis — they form the base of all food chains.

questions.question: What is nitrogen fixation?
options: A) Nitrogen entering the atmosphere
B) Nitrogen being absorbed by plants
C) Conversion of atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia by bacteria
D) Decomposition of protein
questions.explanation: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria (e.g. Rhizobium in root nodules) convert N₂ gas to ammonia — making nitrogen available to plants.

questions.question: What causes eutrophication?
options: A) Drought
B) Excess fertiliser running off into water bodies
C) Acid rain
D) Overfishing
questions.explanation: Fertilisers containing nitrates and phosphates wash into waterways — stimulating algal growth that ultimately depletes oxygen.

questions.question: Why is high biodiversity important?
options: A) It only benefits large animals
B) High biodiversity makes ecosystems more stable and resilient
C) Biodiversity only matters in tropical regions
D) High biodiversity increases competition and reduces productivity
questions.explanation: Diverse ecosystems have more feeding relationships and redundancy — if one species is lost, others can fill its role.

questions.question: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web?
options: A) They are the same
B) A food chain shows one pathway; a food web shows all feeding relationships in an ecosystem
C) Food chains are more complex than food webs
D) Food webs only include decomposers
questions.explanation: A food chain shows a single feeding pathway. A food web shows multiple interconnected food chains — more accurately representing feeding relationships.

questions.question: An ecosystem includes both living and non-living components.
questions.explanation: Biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components interact to form an ecosystem.

questions.question: Decomposers break down dead matter and release nutrients.
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) are essential for nutrient recycling.

questions.question: Eutrophication increases oxygen levels in water.
questions.explanation: Eutrophication DECREASES oxygen levels — decomposing algae use up dissolved oxygen.

questions.question: High biodiversity decreases ecosystem stability.
questions.explanation: High biodiversity INCREASES stability — more species means more feeding relationships and resilience.

questions.question: Producers make their own food by photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Producers are the first trophic level — converting light energy to chemical energy.

questions.question: The carbon cycle only involves photosynthesis and respiration.
questions.explanation: The carbon cycle also involves decomposition, combustion, and the formation/release of fossil fuels.

questions.question: Nitrogen fixation converts atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia.
questions.explanation: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert N₂ to ammonia — making nitrogen available to plants.

questions.question: Eutrophication is caused by a lack of nutrients in water.
questions.explanation: Eutrophication is caused by EXCESS nutrients (fertiliser run-off) — too many nutrients causes algal bloom.

questions.question: All consumers eat plants.
questions.explanation: Herbivores eat plants; carnivores eat animals; omnivores eat both.

questions.question: Food webs are more realistic than food chains.
questions.explanation: Food webs show multiple interconnected feeding relationships — more accurately representing ecosystems.

questions.question: An ________ includes all living organisms and their non-living environment.
questions.explanation: Ecosystems include biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components.

questions.question: ________ break down dead matter — recycling nutrients into the soil.
questions.explanation: Bacteria and fungi are the main decomposers.

questions.question: ________ produces an algal bloom → oxygen depletion → death of aquatic organisms.
questions.explanation: Caused by excess fertiliser (nitrates/phosphates) washing into water.

questions.question: ________ is the variety of different species in an ecosystem.
questions.explanation: High biodiversity increases ecosystem stability and resilience.

questions.question: The ________ cycle moves carbon through organisms, the atmosphere, and the ground.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, and combustion all feature.

questions.question: A ________ makes its own food by photosynthesis — the first trophic level.
questions.explanation: All food chains begin with a producer.

questions.question: ________ fixation converts atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia — done by bacteria.
questions.explanation: Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules fix nitrogen.

questions.question: ________ is the variety of different species — high levels increase stability.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity is threatened by habitat loss, pollution, and climate change.

questions.question: A food ________ shows multiple interconnected feeding relationships.
questions.explanation: Food webs more accurately represent the complexity of ecosystems.

questions.question: ________ contaminate environments — e.g. acid rain, fertiliser run-off, plastics.
questions.explanation: Human activities produce pollutants that damage ecosystems and biodiversity.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: living organisms + non-living environment interacting
breaks down dead matter — recycles nutrients
fertiliser run-off → algal bloom → oxygen depletion
variety of species — high biodiversity = more stable
makes own food by photosynthesis — first trophic level

cards.definition: identical antibodies produced from a single clone of cells — highly specific to one antigen
a cell made by fusing a B lymphocyte with a tumour cell — used to produce monoclonal antibodies
a diagnostic test using monoclonal antibodies to detect specific proteins — e.g. pregnancy tests
monoclonal antibodies carry drugs directly to cancer cells — reducing side effects
monoclonal antibodies used to identify specific pathogens in plants
growing bacteria or fungi in agar plates or broth — requires aseptic technique
procedures to prevent contamination — sterilising equipment, using bunsen burners, working quickly
bacteria that survive antibiotic treatment and reproduce — passing on resistance genes
methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — a bacterium resistant to most antibiotics
viruses inject their genetic material into a host cell — using the cell to make new viruses

questions.question: What are monoclonal antibodies?
options: A) Antibodies made by T cells
B) Identical antibodies from a single clone — highly specific to one antigen
C) Antibodies that attack all pathogens
D) Antibodies produced in bone marrow only
questions.explanation: Monoclonal antibodies are produced by hybridoma cells — they are identical and bind to one specific antigen, making them very precise.

questions.question: What is a hybridoma cell?
options: A) A type of cancer cell
B) A bacterial cell used in ELISA
C) A cell made by fusing a B lymphocyte with a tumour cell — produces monoclonal antibodies
D) A stem cell
questions.explanation: Hybridoma cells combine the antibody-making ability of B lymphocytes with the rapid division of tumour cells — they produce large quantities of identical antibodies.

questions.question: How are monoclonal antibodies used in cancer treatment?
options: A) They destroy all rapidly dividing cells
B) Linked to radioactive markers or drugs — they target and destroy cancer cells specifically
C) They stimulate the immune system generally
D) They prevent cancer cells from dividing by blocking all growth factors
questions.explanation: Targeted drug delivery: monoclonal antibodies carry drugs or toxins directly to cancer cells — minimising damage to healthy cells.

questions.question: What is aseptic technique?
options: A) Sterilising the patient before surgery
B) Procedures to prevent contamination when culturing microorganisms
C) Using antibiotics to kill bacteria
D) Testing antibiotic resistance
questions.explanation: Aseptic technique: sterilise equipment, use Bunsen burners to create an updraught, work quickly, and avoid unnecessary talking to prevent contamination.

questions.question: What is antibiotic resistance?
options: A) The ability of humans to resist antibiotics
B) Bacteria that survive antibiotic treatment and reproduce — passing on resistance genes
C) A type of allergy to antibiotics
D) Antibiotics that no longer work on viruses
questions.explanation: Antibiotic resistance: bacteria with resistance mutations survive treatment, reproduce, and pass on resistance — overuse of antibiotics accelerates this.

questions.question: What is the ELISA test used for?
options: A) Culturing bacteria
B) Detecting antibiotic resistance
C) Detecting specific proteins using monoclonal antibodies — e.g. pregnancy tests, disease diagnosis
D) Growing hybridoma cells
questions.explanation: ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay): monoclonal antibodies bind to a specific antigen — colour change indicates the presence of the target protein.

questions.question: What is MRSA?
options: A) A type of virus
B) A bacterium resistant to most antibiotics — a major hospital infection
C) A fungal infection
D) An antibiotic drug
questions.explanation: MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) is resistant to most antibiotics — it is dangerous in hospitals where patients are vulnerable.

questions.question: How do viruses replicate?
options: A) By binary fission
B) By mitosis
C) By injecting genetic material into a host cell — using the cell's machinery to make new viruses
D) By budding off from the host cell membrane only
questions.explanation: Viruses are not cells — they cannot reproduce independently. They inject their genetic material into a host cell, which produces new viruses that burst out.

questions.question: What makes monoclonal antibodies useful in diagnosis?
options: A) They react with all antigens
B) They are cheap to produce
C) Their high specificity — they bind only to one particular antigen
D) They can cross the blood-brain barrier
questions.explanation: Specificity is the key advantage — monoclonal antibodies bind to one antigen only, making them highly accurate diagnostic tools.

questions.question: Why is antibiotic resistance a global concern?
options: A) Antibiotics are expensive
B) Resistant bacteria cannot be treated with existing antibiotics — infections become untreatable
C) Antibiotics cause side effects
D) Only some bacteria develop resistance
questions.explanation: Antibiotic resistance is evolving faster than new antibiotics are being developed — infections that were once treatable could become fatal.

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies are specific to one antigen.
questions.explanation: Each monoclonal antibody binds to only one particular antigen — making them highly precise.

questions.question: Hybridoma cells are made by fusing two B lymphocytes.
questions.explanation: Hybridoma cells are made by fusing a B lymphocyte with a TUMOUR cell — combining antibody production with rapid division.

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies can deliver drugs directly to cancer cells.
questions.explanation: Targeted drug delivery uses monoclonal antibodies to carry drugs to cancer cells specifically.

questions.question: Aseptic technique is used to ensure all bacteria are killed.
questions.explanation: Aseptic technique PREVENTS CONTAMINATION — it does not necessarily kill bacteria, it prevents unwanted bacteria from entering the culture.

questions.question: Antibiotic resistance is caused by bacteria mutating in response to antibiotics.
questions.explanation: Bacteria don't mutate IN RESPONSE to antibiotics — resistant mutants already exist and are SELECTED by antibiotic exposure.

questions.question: ELISA tests use monoclonal antibodies to detect specific proteins.
questions.explanation: The ELISA test is a highly sensitive diagnostic test — used in pregnancy tests and disease diagnosis.

questions.question: MRSA is resistant to all known treatments.
questions.explanation: MRSA is resistant to most antibiotics but some treatments (e.g. vancomycin) can still be effective.

questions.question: Viruses replicate by binary fission.
questions.explanation: Binary fission is how BACTERIA reproduce. Viruses replicate by injecting genetic material into a host cell.

questions.question: Monoclonal antibody production uses hybridoma cells.
questions.explanation: Hybridoma cells combine the antibody-making ability of B lymphocytes with the immortality of tumour cells.

questions.question: Antibiotic overuse accelerates the development of resistance.
questions.explanation: Overuse selects for resistant bacteria — removing susceptible bacteria and allowing resistant ones to dominate.

questions.question: ________ antibodies are identical antibodies from one clone — specific to one antigen.
questions.explanation: Used in diagnosis, cancer treatment, and research.

questions.question: A ________ cell is made by fusing a B lymphocyte with a tumour cell.
questions.explanation: Hybridoma cells produce large quantities of identical antibodies.

questions.question: The ________ test uses monoclonal antibodies to detect specific proteins.
questions.explanation: Used in pregnancy tests and diagnosis of diseases.

questions.question: ________ drug delivery uses monoclonal antibodies to target cancer cells specifically.
questions.explanation: Reduces side effects by sparing healthy cells.

questions.question: ________ technique prevents contamination when culturing microorganisms.
questions.explanation: Involves sterilising equipment and working in sterile conditions.

questions.question: ________ resistance occurs when bacteria survive antibiotics and pass on resistance genes.
questions.explanation: Natural selection drives antibiotic resistance — resistant bacteria survive and reproduce.

questions.question: ________ is a bacterium resistant to most antibiotics — a major hospital threat.
questions.explanation: MRSA stands for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus.

questions.question: Viruses inject their genetic material into a ________ cell to replicate.
questions.explanation: The host cell's machinery produces new viral particles.

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies are useful diagnostically because of their high ________.
questions.explanation: They bind only to one particular antigen.

questions.question: ________ overuse accelerates the evolution of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.
questions.explanation: New antibiotics must be developed to keep pace with resistance.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: identical — specific to one antigen — from hybridoma cells
B lymphocyte + tumour cell — produces monoclonal antibodies
monoclonal antibodies detect specific proteins — diagnosis
resistant bacteria survive and reproduce — selection
prevents contamination when culturing microorganisms

blocks.block_name: Separate Science: The Eye, Nervous System & Hormones

cards.definition: the transparent front of the eye — refracts (bends) light entering the eye
focuses light onto the retina — changes shape for near and distant objects (accommodation)
the light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye — contains rod and cone cells
the eye changing the shape of the lens to focus on near or distant objects
colour-sensitive cells in the retina — concentrated in the fovea — require bright light
light-sensitive cells in the retina — sensitive in dim light — cannot detect colour
carries electrical impulses from the retina to the brain
a chemical that diffuses across a synapse — e.g. acetylcholine, dopamine
contains oestrogen and/or progesterone — inhibits FSH production — prevents ovulation
fertility treatment — FSH and LH given to stimulate egg production — eggs fertilised outside the body

questions.question: What is the function of the cornea?
options: A) Detects light intensity
B) Focuses light onto the retina
C) Refracts (bends) light entering the eye
D) Contains photoreceptors
questions.explanation: The cornea does most of the eye's light bending — it has a fixed curvature. Fine focusing is done by the lens.

questions.question: What is accommodation?
options: A) The eye adapting to different light levels
B) The lens changing shape to focus on near or distant objects
C) The pupil changing size
D) Rod cells becoming active in dim light
questions.explanation: Accommodation: close objects — the ciliary muscles contract, suspensory ligaments relax, lens becomes fat (more curved). Distant objects — opposite happens.

questions.question: What is the difference between rod and cone cells?
options: A) Rods detect colour; cones work in dim light
B) Rods are sensitive in dim light; cones detect colour in bright light
C) Both detect colour equally
D) Rods are only in the fovea
questions.explanation: Rods: sensitive in dim light, no colour detection, spread across the retina. Cones: colour detection, require bright light, concentrated in the fovea.

questions.question: What is the role of the optic nerve?
options: A) Refracts light
B) Controls the shape of the lens
C) Carries electrical impulses from the retina to the brain
D) Controls pupil size
questions.explanation: The optic nerve carries visual information as electrical impulses to the visual cortex in the brain.

questions.question: How does the contraceptive pill work?
options: A) It prevents sperm reaching the egg
B) It contains oestrogen/progesterone — inhibits FSH — prevents ovulation
C) It makes the uterus lining thick
D) It kills sperm in the reproductive tract
questions.explanation: The combined pill contains oestrogen and progesterone — negative feedback inhibits FSH production, preventing egg maturation and ovulation.

questions.question: What is IVF?
options: A) A contraceptive method
B) A fertility treatment — eggs fertilised outside the body after hormone stimulation
C) A type of hormone therapy for menopause
D) A surgical procedure to unblock fallopian tubes
questions.explanation: IVF: FSH and LH given to stimulate multiple egg production → eggs collected → fertilised with sperm → embryo transferred to uterus.

questions.question: What is a neurotransmitter?
options: A) An electrical impulse
B) A chemical that carries signals across synapses — e.g. acetylcholine
C) A hormone carried in the blood
D) A type of nerve cell
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse across synapses and bind to receptors on the next neurone — converting chemical signals back to electrical ones.

questions.question: Where are cone cells concentrated in the eye?
options: A) Across the whole retina
B) At the edges of the retina
C) In the fovea (yellow spot) — directly behind the lens
D) In the iris
questions.explanation: The fovea has the highest density of cone cells — this is where the most detailed, colour vision occurs. The optic nerve exits nearby creating a blind spot.

questions.question: What does FSH do in IVF treatment?
options: A) Prevents ovulation
B) Stimulates egg production — multiple eggs are collected for fertilisation
C) Thickens the uterus lining
D) Prevents fertilisation
questions.explanation: FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) is given in high doses to stimulate the ovaries to produce multiple eggs — increasing the chance of successful fertilisation.

questions.question: What happens to the lens when focusing on a close object?
options: A) The lens becomes flat (less curved)
B) The ciliary muscles relax
C) The lens becomes fat (more curved) — ciliary muscles contract, suspensory ligaments slacken
D) The pupil dilates
questions.explanation: Close objects: ciliary muscles contract → suspensory ligaments go slack → lens becomes fat and more curved → light refracted more strongly.

questions.question: The cornea does most of the eye's light refraction.
questions.explanation: The cornea has a fixed curvature and does most of the bending of light. The lens fine-tunes focus.

questions.question: Accommodation involves the iris changing size.
questions.explanation: Accommodation involves the LENS changing shape — controlled by ciliary muscles and suspensory ligaments.

questions.question: Rod cells detect colour.
questions.explanation: ROD cells detect light intensity only — they cannot detect colour. CONE cells detect colour.

questions.question: The optic nerve carries impulses from the brain to the retina.
questions.explanation: The optic nerve carries impulses FROM THE RETINA TO THE BRAIN.

questions.question: The contraceptive pill contains hormones that prevent ovulation.
questions.explanation: Oestrogen and progesterone in the pill inhibit FSH — preventing egg maturation.

questions.question: IVF involves fertilisation inside the body.
questions.explanation: IVF stands for in VITRO (outside the body) fertilisation — eggs are fertilised in a laboratory.

questions.question: Neurotransmitters are electrical signals.
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are CHEMICAL signals that diffuse across synapses.

questions.question: Cone cells require dim light to function.
questions.explanation: Cone cells require BRIGHT LIGHT — they detect colour. Rod cells function in dim light.

questions.question: During accommodation for a close object, the lens becomes fatter.
questions.explanation: Ciliary muscles contract → suspensory ligaments relax → lens bulges → greater curvature → more refraction.

questions.question: FSH is used in IVF to stimulate multiple egg production.
questions.explanation: FSH stimulates the ovaries to mature multiple follicles — increasing the number of eggs available.

questions.question: The ________ refracts light entering the eye — does most of the bending.
questions.explanation: The cornea has a fixed curvature — fine focusing is done by the lens.

questions.question: ________ is the lens changing shape to focus on near or distant objects.
questions.explanation: Near: fat lens. Far: flat lens.

questions.question: ________ cells detect colour in bright light — concentrated in the fovea.
questions.explanation: The fovea gives the sharpest, most detailed colour vision.

questions.question: ________ cells detect light in dim conditions — cannot detect colour.
questions.explanation: Rod cells are more widely distributed across the retina.

questions.question: The ________ nerve carries visual impulses from the retina to the brain.
questions.explanation: Where the optic nerve exits, there are no photoreceptors — creating a blind spot.

questions.question: ________ are chemicals that carry signals across synapses.
questions.explanation: E.g. acetylcholine, dopamine, serotonin.

questions.question: The contraceptive ________ inhibits FSH — preventing ovulation.
questions.explanation: Contains oestrogen and/or progesterone — hormonal negative feedback.

questions.question: ________ fertilisation involves eggs being fertilised outside the body.
questions.explanation: IVF uses FSH and LH to stimulate multiple egg production.

questions.question: During accommodation for close objects, ciliary muscles ________.
questions.explanation: Contraction slackens suspensory ligaments — allowing lens to bulge.

questions.question: The fovea has the highest concentration of ________ cells.
questions.explanation: Cone cells are responsible for colour and detailed vision.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: refracts light — fixed curvature — most bending
lens changes shape — near: fat, far: flat
dim light — no colour detection
bright light — colour detection — fovea
eggs fertilised outside body — FSH stimulates egg production
